pitfalls: editorOpen only tolerates ENOENT. Any other fopen failure (permission denied, is-a-directory on some paths, etc.) prints via perror and calls exit(1) immediately -- it is not a soft, recoverable error inside the editor's own UI.

editorOpen's documented int return (0 success / 1 not-found) is dead information at its only call site -- main() never checks it. Don't present the return value as driving any visible behavior; the ENOENT-vs-not branch inside editorOpen itself is what actually matters.

The trailing-newline strip is a single `if`, not a loop, so it removes exactly one byte. Files with CRLF line endings keep a stray '\r' as the last character of every loaded row -- kilo does not normalize CRLF to LF on load.
editorSelectSyntaxHighlight runs on the filename text alone, before editorOpen even attempts to open the file -- syntax mode is chosen whether or not the file exists yet.
There is no 'open with no filename' mode: argc != 2 is a hard usage-and-exit check before any editor state is even initialized, so a filename argument (real or not-yet-existing) is mandatory.

The second `E.dirty = 0` after the read loop is easy to dismiss as a leftover duplicate of the one at the top of the function -- it isn't. Without it, a freshly loaded file would immediately show '(modified)' in the status bar, because editorInsertRow increments E.dirty on every call during the load.

claims.text: editorSave() first flattens the entire in-memory buffer to one heap byte string via editorRowsToString(&len), then opens the file with open(E.filename, O_RDWR|O_CREAT, 0644) -- note there is no O_TRUNC in the flags. It then calls ftruncate(fd, len) to resize the file to the new content's exact length, writes the whole buffer with a single write(fd, buf, len) call, closes the fd, frees buf, resets E.dirty = 0, and reports success with editorSetStatusMessage("%d bytes written on disk", len). It returns 0 on success, 1 on error.

Because the open() flags omit O_TRUNC, ftruncate(fd, len) is not a defensive extra step -- it is load-bearing. Without it, saving shorter content over a longer pre-existing file would leave the old file's trailing bytes (past the new, shorter length) still on disk, since open() alone would neither shrink the file nor overwrite past where the new write() stops.

editorRowsToString(int *buflen) computes totlen as the sum of every row's E.row[j].size+1 (the +1 reserving one byte per row for a trailing '\n'), sets *buflen to that sum (excluding the final NUL), mallocs totlen+1 bytes, then for every row memcpy()s row.chars (the row's raw byte length, row.size) into the buffer and appends a literal '\n' -- for every row, including the last one -- before finally writing one NUL terminator after the loop.

editorSave persists row.chars -- the raw bytes exactly as typed or loaded -- never row.render, the tab-expanded/highlight-annotated buffer editorUpdateRow() builds purely for on-screen display (established in the text-storage chapter). A literal tab character a user typed is written back to disk as a literal tab; the on-screen expansion to spaces has no effect on what lands on disk.

kilo.c's own comment directly above the ftruncate+write pair states the design intent explicitly: "Use truncate + a single write(2) call in order to make saving a bit safer, under the limits of what we can do in a small editor." A single write() call narrows -- but does not close -- the window in which a crash, power loss, or killed process mid-write can corrupt the file; kilo does not implement the more robust write-to-a-temporary-file-then-rename pattern (which is atomic at the filesystem level on POSIX, because rename() replacing an existing file is a single atomic operation) that more defensive editors use. This is a deliberate, named trade-off, not an oversight.

editorSave's single failure path is a `goto writeerr` reached from three distinct checks -- fd == -1 (open failed), ftruncate(fd,len) == -1, or write(fd,buf,len) != len (a short or failed write) -- all funnelling into the same cleanup: free(buf); if (fd != -1) close(fd); editorSetStatusMessage("Can't save! I/O error: %s", strerror(errno)); return 1;. The `if (fd != -1)` guard exists specifically because the open()-failed branch also jumps to writeerr, and close(-1) would itself be an error.

editorProcessKeypress's CTRL_S case is exactly `case CTRL_S: editorSave(); break;` -- one line, no confirmation dialog, no filename prompt, and no branch for saving under a different name. Kilo always writes back to E.filename, the same path editorOpen recorded; there is no 'Save As' anywhere in the program. editorSave()'s int return value is discarded at this, its only call site -- the same pattern the opening-files chapter found with editorOpen's own return value -- so a save's success or failure reaches the user exclusively through the status-bar message editorSave sets itself, not through any control-flow decision in the caller.

E.dirty is reset to 0 only on editorSave's success path, immediately after the write completes -- none of the three writeerr exits touch E.dirty. A failed save therefore correctly leaves the status bar's "(modified)" marker (read from E.dirty in editorRefreshScreen, per the opening-files chapter) still showing, since the in-memory buffer genuinely still has changes the disk copy doesn't.

Quitting with unsaved changes is throttled, not blocked: `#define KILO_QUIT_TIMES 3` and a function-local `static int quit_times = KILO_QUIT_TIMES;` inside editorProcessKeypress. On CTRL_Q, `if (E.dirty && quit_times) { editorSetStatusMessage("WARNING!!! File has unsaved changes. Press Ctrl-Q %d more times to quit.", quit_times); quit_times--; return; }` -- otherwise `exit(0)` runs immediately with no save. Pressing any other key resets `quit_times = KILO_QUIT_TIMES` at the very bottom of editorProcessKeypress (after the switch), so the countdown only tracks *consecutive* Ctrl-Q presses -- typing one character in between resets it back to 3. There is no save-on-quit prompt at all; three (or, if not dirty, zero) consecutive Ctrl-Q presses always exits, discarding any unsaved buffer.

editorFileWasModified(void) is a two-line function -- `return E.dirty;` -- defined near the bottom of kilo.c but never called anywhere else in the file. It is a vestigial public accessor for the dirty flag with no current caller in the codebase, the same 'declared but nothing consults it' shape the opening-files chapter already found in editorOpen's own discarded return value.

editorRowsToString's unconditional trailing '
' after every row, including the last, means kilo's save is not a byte-for-byte echo of whatever was on disk before: a file that had no trailing newline when opened will gain one the moment it's saved. Combined with the opening-files chapter's finding that a CRLF file's stray trailing '' survives inside row.chars (because the load-time strip only removes the final '
'), a CRLF line's saved form becomes row.chars (ending '...') plus the freshly appended '
' -- i.e. '...
' again. The CRLF round-trips correctly, but only as a side effect of two independent, narrowly-scoped pieces of code (a single-byte strip on load, an unconditional '
' append on save) neither of which was written with CRLF handling as its stated purpose -- nothing else in the program (cursor movement, syntax highlighting, rendering) treats that trailing '' as anything but an ordinary character.

open(E.filename, O_RDWR|O_CREAT, 0644) requests rw-r--r-- permissions for a brand-new file, but like any open() call with O_CREAT, the permission bits actually granted are 0644 masked against the calling process's umask -- 0644 is the requested ceiling, not a guaranteed exact mode.

pitfalls: editorSave is not crash-atomic: a crash or power loss between ftruncate() and the completion of write() can leave a shorter or partially-written file on disk. Kilo does not use the write-to-temp-file-then-rename pattern that makes a save atomic at the filesystem level.

The open() flags are O_RDWR|O_CREAT with no O_TRUNC -- skip the ftruncate() call (e.g. in a rewrite exercise) and a shorter save over a longer existing file will leave stale trailing bytes from the old content on disk.
There is no 'Save As': CTRL_S always calls editorSave() with zero arguments, which always writes to E.filename. There is no code path for saving under a different name or path.

editorSave()'s int return value (0/1) is discarded by its only caller, editorProcessKeypress -- exactly like editorOpen's return value in the previous chapter. Don't describe program behavior as branching on it; the status-bar message inside editorSave is the only place success/failure is actually communicated.
editorFileWasModified() is defined but never called anywhere in kilo.c -- don't describe it as part of any active code path; it's dead code.

Ctrl-Q does not block a data-losing quit, it only throttles it: three consecutive Ctrl-Q presses while dirty (or one, if the buffer isn't dirty) always exits with unsaved changes discarded. Typing any other key resets the throttle counter back to 3.

Saved content always comes from row.chars, never row.render -- a reader who conflates the two (easy to do after the text-storage chapter) will wrongly expect tabs to be saved as the spaces they're rendered as on screen.

editorRowsToString appends '\n' after every row unconditionally, including the last -- a file with no trailing newline before it was opened will have one after it's saved. This is a real, if minor, behavior difference from a byte-for-byte copy, worth flagging rather than assuming kilo preserves a file exactly as found.

claims.text: All of Kilo's incremental search lives in one function, editorFind(int fd), under the file's own "Find mode" section header. It is triggered by exactly one case in editorProcessKeypress: `case CTRL_F: editorFind(fd); break;`. There is no separate prompt-drawing helper and no generic callback mechanism (unlike the derivative snaptoken tutorial's editorPrompt()+callback design) -- everything, from reading query characters to running the search to restoring state on exit, is inline in editorFind's own loop.

editorFind opens with `char query[KILO_QUERY_LEN+1] = {0};` (KILO_QUERY_LEN is 256) and `int qlen = 0;`, then immediately saves four fields it will restore on cancel: `int saved_cx = E.cx, saved_cy = E.cy; int saved_coloff = E.coloff, saved_rowoff = E.rowoff;`. It also declares `int last_match = -1;` (the row index of the most recent match, -1 meaning none yet) and `int find_next = 0;` (1 = search forward, -1 = search backward, 0 = no new search this iteration).

editorFind runs its own `while(1)` loop, structurally the same shape as main()'s own `while(1){ editorRefreshScreen(); editorProcessKeypress(fd); }` loop but with the two steps reordered and specialized: each iteration sets a status message showing the query so far via `editorSetStatusMessage("Search: %s (Use ESC/Arrows/Enter)", query)`, calls editorRefreshScreen() so that prompt is actually visible, then reads exactly one key with `editorReadKey(fd)` and decides what to do with it. This is the only other top-level event loop in the whole program besides main()'s -- while it runs, editorProcessKeypress's normal switch (movement, editing, save, quit) is not being called at all; the keys typed while searching are consumed entirely inside editorFind.

Key handling inside the loop, in the order the code checks it: DEL_KEY, CTRL_H, or BACKSPACE shrinks the query by one character (`query[--qlen] = '\0'`, guarded by `qlen != 0`) and resets `last_match = -1` so the next search restarts from the query's new, shorter form. ESC or ENTER both end the loop and return; ESC additionally restores all four saved cursor/scroll fields first (`E.cx = saved_cx; E.cy = saved_cy; E.coloff = saved_coloff; E.rowoff = saved_rowoff;`) while ENTER leaves the cursor exactly where the last match left it -- accepting the found location as the new position. Arrow Right or Arrow Down sets `find_next = 1` (search forward); Arrow Left or Arrow Up sets `find_next = -1` (search backward). Any other key that passes `isprint(c)` is appended to the query (`query[qlen++] = c; query[qlen] = '\0';`, bounded by `qlen < KILO_QUERY_LEN`) and also resets `last_match = -1`.

Both exit paths (ESC and ENTER) run the same two cleanup steps before returning: the FIND_RESTORE_HL macro (below) to undo any temporary match highlighting, and `editorSetStatusMessage("")` to clear the search prompt from the status line -- so the message bar goes blank rather than freezing on the last "Search: ..." text.

After handling the keypress, editorFind decides whether to actually run a search this iteration: `if (last_match == -1) find_next = 1;` forces a forward search the first time (or right after the query changed, since editing also resets last_match to -1), then `if (find_next) { ... }` performs the scan only when a search was actually requested -- meaning a plain arrow key with an unchanged query re-searches from the current match, but typing normally continues to search too, since typing also cleared last_match.

The scan itself is a bounded, wrapping linear walk over every row, starting from last_match and stepping by find_next: `int i, current = last_match; for (i = 0; i < E.numrows; i++) { current += find_next; if (current == -1) current = E.numrows-1; else if (current == E.numrows) current = 0; match = strstr(E.row[current].render, query); if (match) { match_offset = match - E.row[current].render; break; } }`. The loop runs at most E.numrows times, so it always terminates whether or not a match exists anywhere in the file, and the explicit wraparound checks make search cyclic in both directions: searching forward past the last line continues at line 0, and searching backward past line 0 continues at the last line.

Kilo searches E.row[current].render -- the tab-expanded, on-screen text built by editorUpdateRow() -- not E.row[current].chars, the raw bytes typed or loaded. strstr() is a plain, case-sensitive, non-regex substring search; there is no case-insensitive mode, no wildcard or regex support, and no whole-word option anywhere in editorFind.

On a match, editorFind sets `erow *row = &E.row[current]; last_match = current;` and then, only `if (row->hl)` (true in practice for any row, since editorUpdateRow always calls editorUpdateSyntax, which always reallocates and fills row->hl even when there is no active filetype), it saves a private copy of that row's current highlight bytes -- `saved_hl_line = current; saved_hl = malloc(row->rsize); memcpy(saved_hl, row->hl, row->rsize);` -- then overwrites the matched span in place: `memset(row->hl + match_offset, HL_MATCH, qlen);`. HL_MATCH is defined as `#define HL_MATCH 8` and editorSyntaxToColor() maps it to ANSI color 34 (blue): `case HL_MATCH: return 34;`.

The FIND_RESTORE_HL macro undoes exactly that overwrite: `#define FIND_RESTORE_HL do { if (saved_hl) { memcpy(E.row[saved_hl_line].hl, saved_hl, E.row[saved_hl_line].rsize); free(saved_hl); saved_hl = NULL; } } while (0)`. It is called twice inside editorFind: once at the top of the match-handling block, right before evaluating a new match (so only ever one row's highlighting is patched at a time -- moving to a new match first restores the previous one), and once more on the way out through the ESC/ENTER exit path (so no leftover blue highlight survives after search mode ends).

On a match, editorFind repositions the viewport directly rather than through editorMoveCursor: `E.cy = 0; E.cx = match_offset; E.rowoff = current; E.coloff = 0;`. Since the on-screen row is `E.rowoff + E.cy`, setting E.cy to 0 and E.rowoff to the matched row's index scrolls the file so the matched line always lands on the very first visible screen row, regardless of where it was on screen before -- a different scrolling behavior from ordinary cursor movement (editorMoveCursor), which only scrolls the minimum needed to keep the cursor in view. E.coloff is unconditionally reset to 0 as part of the same block.

Immediately after that, editorFind does its own horizontal-scroll check for a match past the right edge: `if (E.cx > E.screencols) { int diff = E.cx - E.screencols; E.cx -= diff; E.coloff += diff; }` -- which reduces to E.cx being set to exactly E.screencols and E.coloff absorbing the rest. This differs from the equivalent, already-established clamp in editorMoveCursor (Chapter 09), which checks `E.cx > E.screencols-1` and clamps to `E.cx = E.screencols-1` -- one column short of editorFind's result. Since Chapter 09 established `[0, E.screencols)` as the valid on-screen column range for E.cx, editorFind's version can leave E.cx one column past the last valid screen column in this specific overflow branch.

editorFind sets E.cx directly to match_offset, an index into E.row[current].render (the tab-expanded string). Everywhere else in the program that positions the cursor for drawing -- the inline cx-to-screen-column loop inside editorRefreshScreen documented in the text-storage chapter -- treats E.cx as an offset counted against E.row[filerow].chars (the raw, un-expanded bytes) starting from E.coloff, expanding tabs as it walks. For a query match on a line whose raw text before the match contains one or more tab characters, render's expanded index and chars's raw index diverge (a tab can consume up to 8 columns in render but is one byte in chars), so E.cx ends up counted in the wrong unit for that row -- a narrow, real inconsistency between how search locates a match and how the rest of the editor interprets E.cx, not a documented feature.

The startup help message printed once in main(), before the event loop even starts, already advertises the feature: `editorSetStatusMessage("HELP: Ctrl-S = save | Ctrl-Q = quit | Ctrl-F = find");` -- confirming Ctrl-F as find's only entry point and that it ships alongside save and quit as one of the editor's three headline keybindings.

editorFind takes no query argument and returns void; it is a blocking, modal call -- editorProcessKeypress's CTRL_F case calls it and does not resume its own switch statement until editorFind's internal loop itself returns via ESC or ENTER. Kilo has no asynchronous or background search; the whole editor is unresponsive to anything except the keys editorFind explicitly handles for the duration of a search.

pitfalls: editorFind is a single, monolithic function with its own inline while(1) loop -- there is no editorPrompt()+callback abstraction here (that pattern belongs to the derivative snaptoken tutorial, not antirez's original kilo.c). Don't describe the search prompt as reusing a generic prompt helper; kilo.c has none.
Search runs against E.row[].render (the tab-expanded, on-screen text), not E.row[].chars (the raw bytes) -- and it's a plain strstr() substring match: case-sensitive, no regex, no whole-word option.

While editorFind's loop is running, editorProcessKeypress's normal switch (movement, save, quit, editing) does not run at all -- search is fully modal. The only keys that do anything are backspace/delete, ESC, ENTER, the four arrow keys, and printable characters for the query.

Only ESC restores the pre-search cursor and scroll position (saved_cx/cy/coloff/rowoff); ENTER accepts wherever the last match left the cursor. Conflating the two exit keys is a real behavioral difference, not a style choice.

Search always scrolls the matched line to the very top of the screen (E.cy = 0; E.rowoff = current), unlike ordinary cursor movement which only scrolls the minimum amount needed to keep the cursor visible (Chapter 09's editorMoveCursor). Don't describe search's scrolling as reusing editorMoveCursor -- it sets E.rowoff/E.cy directly instead.

editorFind's own horizontal-scroll clamp on a match past the right edge checks `E.cx > E.screencols` and lands E.cx at exactly E.screencols -- one column past the `[0, E.screencols)` valid range Chapter 09 established, where the equivalent check in editorMoveCursor uses `E.cx > E.screencols-1` and clamps to `E.screencols-1`. Treat this as a narrow, real inconsistency in the source worth naming, not as intentional or as a mistake to gloss over.

match_offset is an index into render (post-tab-expansion) but gets assigned straight to E.cx, which the rest of the program (the cursor-drawing loop in editorRefreshScreen) treats as an offset into chars (pre-expansion) counted from E.coloff. On a line with tabs before the match, these two indices are not the same number -- a subtle correctness gap specific to tabbed lines, not a general search bug.

The temporary blue match highlight (HL_MATCH) is a real, if narrow, mutation of row->hl -- it is saved and restored via the FIND_RESTORE_HL macro, but only for one row at a time (saved_hl_line is a single slot, not a stack), and only while editorFind's own loop is running. If a reader imagines it as reusing the syntax-highlighting engine, be precise: it's a one-off memset over the same array editorUpdateSyntax fills, not a new highlight category the syntax highlighter itself knows about.

claims.text: erow.hl is declared `unsigned char *hl;` with the comment "Syntax highlight type for each character in render." -- it is a parallel array to render (the tab-expanded, on-screen buffer), one byte per rendered character, not one byte per raw chars byte.

Kilo's syntax-highlight types are a flat set of #define constants, not an enum: HL_NORMAL=0, HL_NONPRINT=1, HL_COMMENT=2 (single-line), HL_MLCOMMENT=3 (multi-line), HL_KEYWORD1=4, HL_KEYWORD2=5, HL_STRING=6, HL_NUMBER=7, HL_MATCH=8. Two further bit-flag constants, HL_HIGHLIGHT_STRINGS (1<<0) and HL_HIGHLIGHT_NUMBERS (1<<1), live in a per-language editorSyntax record's `flags` field so a language definition can opt in or out of string/number coloring independently of keyword coloring.

editorUpdateSyntax(erow *row) is the single function that fills row->hl, and it is called unconditionally as the last line of editorUpdateRow() -- the same function (established in Chapter 08) that every text-changing operation already calls to rebuild render from chars. There is no separate 'now go highlight' step and no incremental per-keystroke patch: every edit that touches a row causes that row's entire hl array to be thrown away and recomputed from scratch, left to right, in one pass.

editorUpdateSyntax's first two statements are `row->hl = realloc(row->hl,row->rsize); memset(row->hl,HL_NORMAL,row->rsize);` -- then `if (E.syntax == NULL) return;`, with the comment "No syntax, everything is HL_NORMAL." Plain-text files (or any file whose name matched no entry in the highlight-rules table) take this early return: 'no highlighting' isn't a separate code path, it's this same function stopping one line in.

The scan that fills the rest of row->hl carries exactly three pieces of state as it walks the row left to right: `prev_sep` (was the immediately preceding character a token boundary), `in_string` (0, or the quote character currently open, '"' or '\''), and `in_comment` (inside an unterminated /* */ span carried over from a previous row or opened on this one). There is no token list and no tree -- classifying a character is a decision made from these three scalars plus the character itself, on the fly.

is_separator(c) returns true for the NUL terminator, any isspace() character, or a character found in the literal set ",.()+-/*=~%[];" -- it is the one shared definition of 'a token just ended here' that the scanner consults before testing for a keyword, and before deciding whether a digit continues a number (`isdigit(*p) && (prev_sep || row->hl[i-1]==HL_NUMBER)`). Chapter 15's keyword/number/string classification rules are built directly on this same primitive, not a separate one.

Multi-line comment state has to survive across row boundaries, so erow carries a persistent `int hl_oc` ("Row had open comment at end in last syntax highlight check"). editorRowHasOpenComment(row) computes the true/false value: it checks that the row's last hl byte is HL_MLCOMMENT *and* that the row's actual last two rendered characters are not literally "*/" -- guarding against the case where a multi-line comment closes exactly at end-of-line (its closing two bytes are still colored HL_MLCOMMENT, but the row is not, in fact, still open). editorUpdateSyntax reads the *previous* row's editorRowHasOpenComment() result to decide whether the current row should start already inside a comment.

When rehighlighting a row changes whether that row ends with an open comment, editorUpdateSyntax recurses onto the next row: `int oc = editorRowHasOpenComment(row); if (row->hl_oc != oc && row->idx+1 < E.numrows) editorUpdateSyntax(&E.row[row->idx+1]); row->hl_oc = oc;` -- kilo.c's own comment above this block: "Propagate syntax change to the next row if the open comment state changed. This may recursively affect all the following rows in the file." A single keystroke that opens or closes a /* */ span can therefore cascade a full rehighlight through every row below it, not just the edited line.

editorSyntaxToColor(int hl) is a pure switch mapping a highlight type to an ANSI SGR foreground color number: HL_COMMENT and HL_MLCOMMENT both return 36 (cyan), HL_KEYWORD1 returns 33 (yellow), HL_KEYWORD2 returns 32 (green), HL_STRING returns 35 (magenta), HL_NUMBER returns 31 (red), HL_MATCH returns 34 (blue), and the default case returns 37 (white). In practice the default branch never fires through normal rendering, because the row-drawing loop in editorRefreshScreen special-cases HL_NORMAL and HL_NONPRINT before it would ever call editorSyntaxToColor.

Turning classified bytes into an actual colored terminal line is entirely the job of the per-row drawing loop inside editorRefreshScreen (editorUpdateSyntax only classifies; it never writes an escape sequence). For each on-screen character j of the visible slice, it branches three ways: hl[j]==HL_NONPRINT wraps a substituted display character in reverse video (`\x1b[7m` ... `\x1b[0m`); hl[j]==HL_NORMAL resets the foreground to terminal-default (`\x1b[39m`) but only if a non-default color was currently active; anything else calls editorSyntaxToColor(hl[j]) and, only if that color differs from a tracked `current_color`, emits one `\x1b[<n>m` escape before the character.

The current_color tracking inside that loop is a deliberate optimization: a run of same-colored characters (a whole keyword, a whole string literal) shares a single leading color escape rather than repeating one before every character. It's the same batching instinct Chapters 05/06 established for the append buffer (accumulate, then flush once) applied specifically to color escapes -- and after each row, the loop unconditionally appends `\x1b[39m` (reset foreground) then `\x1b[0K` (erase to end of line) before the `\r\n`, so no color or stray glyph ever bleeds from one row's drawing into the next.

Which highlight rules apply to a file at all is decided exactly once, before any row exists: main() calls `editorSelectSyntaxHighlight(argv[1])` -- which searches a fixed table of language definitions for a filename match and sets the global `E.syntax` pointer (or leaves it NULL) -- *before* editorOpen() reads the file's lines. Because editorInsertRow() (called once per line during that initial load) already ends in editorUpdateRow()/editorUpdateSyntax(), every row is highlighted correctly against the right E.syntax from the very first paint; there is no second, later pass that goes back and colors an already-loaded file. (The language-definition table itself -- filematch patterns, keyword lists, comment markers -- is Chapter 16's subject, not this one's.)

HL_MATCH (8) is defined in this same block of constants and consumed by editorSyntaxToColor and the drawing loop exactly like any other highlight type, but editorUpdateSyntax itself never assigns it -- Chapter 13 already showed editorFind writing HL_MATCH directly into row->hl with a raw memset(). The classifier and the colorer both treat HL_MATCH as ordinary data; only one specific external caller ever produces it.

pitfalls: row->hl is not a token stream or an AST -- it's one byte per *rendered* character, and the 'parser' filling it is a single forward scan carrying three scalars (prev_sep, in_string, in_comment), not a lexer producing token objects.

hl indexes align with render (the tab-expanded buffer), the same chars/render split Chapter 08 established -- a highlight byte at index i colors render[i], not chars[i]. On a line with tabs before a colored span, the two indices diverge, exactly the same class of gap Chapter 13 found between search's match_offset and E.cx.

Rehighlighting is not scoped to the line just edited. Because of the multi-line-comment propagation, one keystroke that opens or closes a /* */ span can recursively rehighlight every row below it in the file -- most of those recursive calls return almost immediately because hl_oc doesn't change, but an edit that flips comment state near the top of a large file is a real, if narrow, cost worth naming rather than glossing over.

editorSyntaxToColor's default (37, white) reads like the answer for HL_NORMAL, but it never actually executes for that case in ordinary rendering -- editorRefreshScreen's drawing loop intercepts HL_NORMAL (and HL_NONPRINT) with their own branches before a color lookup would ever happen.

HL_MATCH is defined and colored right alongside the 'real' syntax categories, but editorUpdateSyntax never produces it -- only editorFind (Chapter 13) does, by writing directly into row->hl. Don't describe HL_MATCH as something the highlighter 'detects'; it's a value the highlighter's storage merely agrees to display.

editorRowHasOpenComment's guard against a comment that closes exactly at end-of-line (checking the literal last two rendered characters aren't '*/', not just the last hl byte's type) is easy to skim past -- without it, any line ending in the comment's own closer would be misdiagnosed as still-open and incorrectly carry comment coloring onto the next row.

claims.text: Kilo's language definitions live in a struct editorSyntax: char **extensions (a NULL-terminated list of matching filename suffixes), char **keywords (a NULL-terminated list of keyword strings), a 2-byte singleline_comment_start, 3-byte multiline_comment_start and multiline_comment_end, and an int flags (HL_HIGHLIGHT_STRINGS / HL_HIGHLIGHT_NUMBERS bit flags). The C/C++ entry sets extensions to {".c",".h",".cpp",".hpp",".cc",NULL}, comment delimiters to "//", "/*", "*/", and both highlight flags on.

C_HL_keywords[] is one flat NULL-terminated array holding three logical groups back to back, with no struct-level separation between them: C keywords (auto, break, case, continue, default, do, else, enum, extern, for, goto, if, register, return, sizeof, static, struct, switch, typedef, union, volatile, while, NULL), C++ keywords (alignas, and, class, constexpr, delete, namespace, template, virtual, and more), and finally C/C++ type names (int, long, double, float, char, unsigned, signed, void, short, auto, const, bool) each written with a trailing pipe, e.g. "int|".

The trailing '|' on a type-name keyword string is the entire mechanism that distinguishes 'type' from 'other keyword' at scan time. Inside the keyword-matching loop: `int klen = strlen(keywords[j]); int kw2 = keywords[j][klen-1] == '|'; if (kw2) klen--;` -- kw2 is a boolean read off the last character, and klen is then shortened by one so the pipe itself is never compared against the row's text or written into hl.

A matched keyword is painted in one memset, not a per-character loop: `memset(row->hl+i,kw2 ? HL_KEYWORD2 : HL_KEYWORD1,klen);` -- so 'int' (an HL_KEYWORD2/type, green) and 'return' (an HL_KEYWORD1/keyword, yellow) are colored by the same line of code, branching only on which list entry matched.

The keyword-match test itself is `if (!memcmp(p,keywords[j],klen) && is_separator(*(p+klen)))` -- both conditions are required. memcmp alone would let 'int' match the start of 'internal'; the is_separator check on the character immediately after the candidate match is what rejects that, by requiring the keyword to be immediately followed by a token boundary (or end of string).

Keyword matching is gated behind prev_sep: `if (prev_sep) { ... for (j = 0; keywords[j]; j++) { ... } if (keywords[j] != NULL) { prev_sep = 0; continue; } }`. It only even attempts the keyword table when the scan is sitting at the start of a new token (prev_sep true) -- so 'reprintf' is never tested against 'printf' starting mid-word, because prev_sep would be false partway through 'reprintf'.

The keyword loop breaks out of the for-loop the instant it finds a match (`break;` right after the memset), then tests `if (keywords[j] != NULL)` after the loop to tell 'the loop broke because of a match' apart from 'the loop ran off the end of the NULL-terminated list without matching anything' -- the same NULL-terminated-array idiom, re-used for control flow rather than just iteration.

Kilo's number rule accepts a digit as the start of a number only if it's at a token boundary or continues a run already classified as a number, and separately accepts a literal '.' as a continuation of a number in progress: `if ((isdigit(*p) && (prev_sep || row->hl[i-1] == HL_NUMBER)) || (*p == '.' && i>0 && row->hl[i-1] == HL_NUMBER)) { row->hl[i] = HL_NUMBER; p++; i++; prev_sep = 0; continue; }`. It advances and colors exactly one character per loop iteration here, unlike the keyword branch's one-shot memset.

The number rule has no notion of a number literal as a unit -- there is no lookahead, no separate float-vs-int state, and no bounds check against `i-1` being negative because prev_sep (true at the very start of a row) covers that case; hl[i-1] is only read on the second and later iterations of a digit run, when i is already >0.

String handling toggles a two-state machine stored directly in the scan's own in_string variable, which holds 0 (not in a string) or the exact opening quote character ('"' or '\''): entering sets `in_string = *p`; inside a string, a backslash consumes itself and the following character together (`if (*p == '\\') { row->hl[i+1] = HL_STRING; p += 2; i += 2; prev_sep = 0; continue; }`) so an escaped quote can never be mistaken for the closing quote; the string ends only when the current character equals in_string itself (`if (*p == in_string) in_string = 0;`), meaning a '"' cannot close a string opened with '\'' or vice versa.

Kilo's escape handling inside strings is purely structural, not semantic: the backslash-escape branch advances past any two characters as a pair and colors both HL_STRING, whether or not the second character is a recognized C escape (\n, \t, \\, \", or something else entirely, even a genuinely invalid escape) -- the highlighter is not validating the string's contents, only refusing to let a backslash-quote end the string early.

Single-line comments are detected with `if (prev_sep && *p == scs[0] && *(p+1) == scs[1])` (scs is E.syntax->singleline_comment_start, "//" for C) and, once matched, the rest of the row is colored in one shot and the function returns immediately: `memset(row->hl+i,HL_COMMENT,row->size-i); return;` -- note this is `row->size` (chars length), not row->rsize (render length), and note the bare `return`, which skips the multi-line-comment-state propagation logic at the bottom of editorUpdateSyntax entirely for this row.

Multi-line comment open/continue/close are three separate branches ahead of the string/number/keyword checks, so they take priority: already-in_comment colors the current char HL_MLCOMMENT and checks for the two-character closer (mce, "*/" for C) to clear in_comment; not-yet-in-comment checks for the two-character opener (mcs, "/*" for C) at prev_sep-independent position (no prev_sep gate on entering a /* comment, unlike keywords) and enters in_comment, coloring both opener characters HL_MLCOMMENT immediately.

The single-line-comment check runs on every loop iteration (not gated to only run once) but is naturally guarded by prev_sep and by the fact that once it fires it returns from the whole function -- so '//' appearing after code on the same line (e.g. `x = 1; // comment`) is still caught correctly, exactly at the point the scan reaches it, because prev_sep is true right after the preceding ';' and space.

Non-printable bytes are checked with `if (!isprint(*p)) { row->hl[i] = HL_NONPRINT; p++; i++; prev_sep = 0; continue; }`, positioned after string/comment handling but before the number and keyword checks -- so a non-printable byte inside a string or comment is still colored as part of that string/comment (those branches `continue` before reaching this check), while one in plain code gets its own HL_NONPRINT byte and is later substituted with a caret-letter or '?' by the drawing loop (Chapter 14).

Every branch in the scan body ends by either falling through to `prev_sep = is_separator(*p); p++; i++;` at the loop's bottom (the 'ordinary character' path) or by its own explicit p++/i++/continue and prev_sep update -- there is no branch that advances p without ever touching prev_sep, which is what keeps 'start of a new token' accurate on every iteration, including immediately after a string, comment, number, or keyword ends.

pitfalls: The trailing '|' on a keyword string (e.g. "int|") is not part of the identifier -- it's a flag read off the last byte at scan time to pick HL_KEYWORD2 (types, green) over HL_KEYWORD1 (keywords, yellow), then stripped from klen before comparison. Describing C_HL_keywords as 'just a list of keywords' misses that it's really two lists interleaved by a one-character sigil.

memcmp alone would let 'int' match inside 'internal' or 'interval'. It's the is_separator(*(p+klen)) check immediately after -- requiring a token boundary right after the candidate match -- that rejects a keyword matching as a prefix of a longer identifier.

Keyword and number matching both depend on prev_sep, but comment/string detection does not gate multi-line-comment-open on prev_sep -- meaning `x/*comment*/` (no space before the /*) still opens a comment correctly, since the mcs check runs unconditionally once not already in_comment, while a would-be keyword glued directly onto other text is correctly rejected because prev_sep is false there.

The single-line-comment branch measures to row->size (the chars-length field), not row->rsize (the render/tab-expanded length) like every other branch in this function -- a subtle inconsistency in the source itself, worth flagging rather than silently correcting when walking through the code.

Backslash-escape handling inside strings is purely structural (advance two characters, color both HL_STRING) -- it does not validate that the escaped character is a real C escape sequence. \q would be accepted and colored exactly like \n; the highlighter's job is only to not let \" end a string early, not to lint escape sequences.

The number rule colors a lone '.' as HL_NUMBER only when it immediately follows a byte already classified HL_NUMBER (i>0 && row->hl[i-1]==HL_NUMBER) -- so a standalone '.' (a struct member access, e.g. `p.x`) is never colored as a number, only a '.' that continues a digit run like '3.14'.

Number detection has no unary-minus or exponent handling: '-5' colors only the '5' as HL_NUMBER (the '-' is punctuation/a separator character, not part of the number rule), and '1e10' would color '1', stop at 'e' (keyword-gated, doesn't match anything, falls to 'not special chars'), then not resume number coloring at '10' because prev_sep is false and hl[i-1] isn't HL_NUMBER at that point -- a real, demonstrable gap in the scanner worth walking through as an edge case, not a hidden detail.

The keyword for-loop's post-loop test `if (keywords[j] != NULL)` only works because of the break statement inside the loop on a match; without that break, j would always end at the NULL sentinel and the check would never detect a match. It's easy to misread the two together as redundant when they're actually load-bearing on each other.

claims.text: The full struct editorSyntax (kilo.c ~lines 71-78) has six fields: `char **filematch` (a NULL-terminated list of filename-matching patterns), `char **keywords` (a NULL-terminated list of keyword strings, the same table Chapter 15 walked), `char singleline_comment_start[2]`, `char multiline_comment_start[3]`, `char multiline_comment_end[3]` (fixed-size C string buffers, not pointers, each sized for exactly a 2-character delimiter plus its null terminator), and `int flags` (the HL_HIGHLIGHT_STRINGS / HL_HIGHLIGHT_NUMBERS bit flags introduced in Chapter 14). Every field of this struct is something Chapters 14-15 already used through E.syntax -- this chapter is about the two fields that decide *which* struct instance E.syntax points to and *how many* instances exist: filematch, and the HLDB array itself.

kilo.c documents its own extension mechanism in a comment block directly above the HLDB definitions (~lines 143-162), addressed to a future maintainer in the imperative: 'In order to add a new syntax, define two arrays with a list of file name matches and keywords... Finally add a stanza in the HLDB global variable with two arrays of strings, and a set of flags in order to enable highlighting of comments and numbers... The characters for single and multi line comments must be exactly two and must be provided as well... There is no support to highlight patterns currently.' This is the author's own three-step recipe, not an inferred one: (1) a filematch array, (2) a keywords array, (3) a new HLDB stanza combining them with the four delimiter strings and the flags.

HLDB is declared as a plain C array of struct editorSyntax with exactly one initialized element -- the C/C++ entry -- and its size is never hardcoded elsewhere: `#define HLDB_ENTRIES (sizeof(HLDB)/sizeof(HLDB[0]))` computes the element count from the array's byte size divided by one element's byte size, at compile time. Adding a second `{ ... }` stanza to the HLDB initializer is the entire change needed for HLDB_ENTRIES, and every loop that uses it, to see the new entry -- no separate 'how many entries' constant exists to forget to update.

editorSelectSyntaxHighlight(char *filename) (kilo.c ~lines 533-551) is the single function that turns a filename string into a decision. It loops `for (unsigned int j = 0; j < HLDB_ENTRIES; j++)`, and for each HLDB entry `s`, loops its filematch array `while(s->filematch[i])`, testing `strstr(filename, s->filematch[i])`. The moment a pattern matches (subject to the dot-prefix rule below), it sets `E.syntax = s` and returns immediately -- the first matching entry, scanning HLDB in array order and each entry's filematch patterns in array order, wins; there is no 'best match' or specificity ranking.

The dot-prefix rule is the one piece of real logic in the match test: `if ((p = strstr(filename,s->filematch[i])) != NULL) { if (s->filematch[i][0] != '.' || p[patlen] == '\0') { E.syntax = s; return; } }`. strstr alone finds a pattern *anywhere* inside the filename string, as a plain substring -- which is exactly what's wanted for a non-extension pattern like `"Makefile"` (the doc comment's own example: match anywhere in the name). But for a pattern that starts with `.` (an extension), a bare substring match is too loose -- `".c"` is a substring of `foo.copy` too. The extra check `p[patlen] == '\0'` requires that nothing follows the match inside the filename, i.e. the pattern must land at the very end of the string. So `.c` matches `main.c` (nothing after the match) but not `foo.copy` (the match at index 3 is followed by `opy`), and a pattern with no leading dot skips this extra check entirely and matches as a plain substring anywhere in the name.

editorSelectSyntaxHighlight is called exactly once per run, from main(), as `editorSelectSyntaxHighlight(argv[1])` -- before `editorOpen(argv[1])` reads a single line of the file. E.syntax is therefore already correctly set (to a real entry or to whatever it was initialized to if no pattern matches) before editorInsertRow()/editorUpdateRow()/editorUpdateSyntax() run for the first time during the initial load; there is no separate 'detect the language, then go back and re-highlight' pass. E.syntax itself is initialized to NULL in initEditor(), so a filename that matches no HLDB entry's filematch list leaves E.syntax NULL, which Chapter 14 already established makes editorUpdateSyntax return after one line ('No syntax, everything is HL_NORMAL.').

Kilo ships with exactly one HLDB stanza (C/C++), so in the real, unmodified kilo.c, editorSelectSyntaxHighlight's outer loop runs at most once (HLDB_ENTRIES == 1) -- the general two-level loop structure (entries, then each entry's filematch list) is fully general machinery sized for a table of one. Adding a second language is purely additive: a new filematch array, a new keywords array, one new HLDB stanza -- editorSelectSyntaxHighlight, editorUpdateSyntax, and the drawing loop all need zero changes, because they were already written generically against 'whatever E.syntax happens to point at.'

The doc comment's own words -- 'There is no support to highlight patterns currently' -- is an explicit, named limitation of the filematch mechanism: it is plain substring/suffix matching only, with no glob (`*.py`), no regex, and no case-insensitivity handling beyond whatever strstr does (which is case-sensitive). A file named `MAIN.C` would not match `.c`, and there is no way to express 'anything ending in one of several extensions' in a single pattern -- each extension needs its own entry in the filematch array, exactly as C_HL_extensions lists `.c`, `.h`, `.cpp`, `.hpp`, and `.cc` as five separate strings rather than one pattern.

The comment/delimiter fields have a hard, undocumented-in-the-struct-but-documented-in-the-comment size constraint: 'The characters for single and multi line comments must be exactly two.' This matches the struct's own fixed buffer sizes (`singleline_comment_start[2]`, `multiline_comment_start[3]`, `multiline_comment_end[3]` -- 2 content bytes plus a null terminator each), which in turn matches exactly how editorUpdateSyntax's scan reads them: always as a fixed pair, `scs[0]`/`scs[1]`, `mcs[0]`/`mcs[1]`, `mce[0]`/`mce[1]`, never with a strlen() call. A language whose natural comment marker is one character (like `#` for Python or shell) or three-plus characters cannot be expressed as-is in this struct without either padding or changing the struct and the scan that reads it -- a real constraint on 'extend it to more languages,' not just a style note.

Because HL_HIGHLIGHT_STRINGS and HL_HIGHLIGHT_NUMBERS are independent bits in one `flags` int, a new language entry can opt into either, both, or neither, independent of its keyword list -- e.g. a hypothetical config-file syntax could set only HL_HIGHLIGHT_STRINGS (color quoted values) while leaving numbers uncolored, just by omitting HL_HIGHLIGHT_NUMBERS from the bitwise-OR in its stanza. (Kilo's shipped C/C++ entry sets both.) Note, however, that editorUpdateSyntax's shipped scan body (Chapter 15) does not actually branch on these flags in the version fetched for this book -- the flags are read by the struct/table design but the C entry always sets both, so this is the documented *intended* mechanism rather than something demonstrated by a second, differently-flagged entry in the shipped source.

snippets.code:
/* =========================== Syntax highlights DB =========================
 *
 * In order to add a new syntax, define two arrays with a list of file name
 * matches and keywords. The file name matches are used in order to match
 * a given syntax with a given file name: if a match pattern starts with a
 * dot, it is matched as the last past of the filename, for example ".c".
 * Otherwise the pattern is just searched inside the filenme, like "Makefile").
 *
 * The list of keywords to highlight is just a list of words, however if they
 * a trailing '|' character is added at the end, they are highlighted in
 * a different color, so that you can have two different sets of keywords.
 *
 * Finally add a stanza in the HLDB global variable with two two arrays
 * of strings, and a set of flags in order to enable highlighting of
 * comments and numbers.
 *
 * The characters for single and multi line comments must be exactly two
 * and must be provided as well (see the C language example).
 *
 * There is no support to highlight patterns currently. */

pitfalls: filematch matching is not glob or regex -- it's strstr() plus one special case. A pattern starting with '.' must match at the very end of the filename (protects '.c' from matching 'foo.copy'); a pattern with no leading dot matches as a substring anywhere in the filename (the doc comment's own example is 'Makefile'). There is no wildcard, and the doc comment says so explicitly: 'There is no support to highlight patterns currently.'

Multiple extensions for one language are multiple separate filematch entries, not one pattern -- C_HL_extensions lists five strings (.c, .h, .cpp, .hpp, .cc), each checked independently by the while(s->filematch[i]) loop, not a single '.c/.h/.cpp' pattern.

The comment-delimiter fields are fixed at exactly two content characters each (singleline_comment_start[2], multiline_comment_start[3], multiline_comment_end[3] -- 2 chars + NUL). This is a real structural limit, not a style guideline: a one-character comment marker (# for Python/shell) or a longer one doesn't fit the struct as shipped without changing both the struct and the scan in Chapter 15 that reads scs[0]/scs[1] as a fixed pair.

editorSelectSyntaxHighlight stops at the first match, scanning HLDB entries in array order and each entry's filematch patterns in array order -- if two hypothetical entries could both match the same filename, whichever is listed first in HLDB wins; there is no 'most specific pattern' tiebreaking.

Selection happens once, from argv[1], before the file is even opened -- there is no re-detection if the buffer's content later looks like a different language, and no way to select a syntax for a file opened with no filename argument at all (E.syntax stays NULL, exactly like an unrecognized extension).

claims.text: kilo.c has no single centralized die()/fatal() helper (unlike the popular snaptoken tutorial fork of Kilo). Instead each call site that detects a fatal condition calls perror()/prints a message and exit()s directly, or uses a local 'goto fatal'/'goto writeerr' label for its own cleanup before returning an error code.

disableRawMode(fd) checks E.rawmode (a global flag) before calling tcsetattr(fd, TCSAFLUSH, &orig_termios) to restore the terminal, and does not check tcsetattr's return value — the comment in the source says 'Don't even check the return value as it's too late,' since there is nothing further the program could do differently if restoring the terminal fails during exit.

editorAtExit(void) is a one-line wrapper that calls disableRawMode(STDIN_FILENO); it is registered with atexit(editorAtExit) from inside enableRawMode(), not from main(), and only once raw mode is actually about to be turned on.
enableRawMode(fd) registers atexit(editorAtExit) before it calls tcgetattr/tcsetattr, so even if the raw-mode setup itself fails partway through, the exit handler is already armed.

enableRawMode(fd) returns 0 immediately if E.rawmode is already 1 (idempotent), returns -1 with errno set to ENOTTY via a 'goto fatal' label if isatty(STDIN_FILENO) is false or if tcgetattr/tcsetattr fail, and otherwise sets E.rawmode = 1 and returns 0.

main() calls enableRawMode(STDIN_FILENO) without checking its return value, so if raw mode setup fails (e.g. stdin is not a TTY), the editor's main loop still runs as though it succeeded — editorReadKey() then reads from a terminal still in canonical/cooked mode (line-buffered, echoing) instead of raw mode, and users can be confused why keystrokes don't behave as expected until Enter is pressed.

In main(), the call order is initEditor(); editorSelectSyntaxHighlight(argv[1]); editorOpen(argv[1]); enableRawMode(STDIN_FILENO). Because editorOpen() runs before enableRawMode(), a fatal error inside editorOpen() (perror + exit(1) on an unreadable existing file) always happens while the terminal is still in its normal (cooked) mode — there is no raw-mode terminal state to restore at that point, so skipping cleanup there is safe by construction, not by accident.

editorOpen(filename) treats a missing file (fopen fails with errno == ENOENT) as a normal, recoverable case: it returns 1 and lets the caller continue (the editor opens on a fresh, empty, to-be-created file). Any other fopen failure (e.g. permission denied) is treated as fatal: it calls perror('Opening file') and exit(1).

editorReadKey() loops on read(fd,&c,1) while it returns 0 (a VTIME timeout with no byte read, per the raw-mode VMIN=0/VTIME=1 configuration), and calls exit(1) immediately if read() returns -1 (a genuine I/O error on the terminal). Because this happens after enableRawMode() has already run, the registered atexit(editorAtExit) fires and disableRawMode() restores the terminal before the process actually dies.

editorUpdateRow() computes the allocation size needed for a row's rendered (tab-expanded, non-printable-substituted) representation as an unsigned long long, and if that size would exceed UINT32_MAX it prints 'Some line of the edited file is too long for kilo' with printf and calls exit(1) rather than proceeding with a malloc() call whose size argument would silently truncate/wrap on a narrower type.

None of kilo.c's malloc()/realloc() calls (E.row, row->chars, row->render, row->hl, the append buffer, editorRowsToString's buffer, E.filename, etc.) check for a NULL return; the code assumes allocation always succeeds. This is a real, acknowledged limitation of a program optimized for brevity ('less than 1K lines'), not an oversight the tutorial hides.

editorSave() writes with a 'safer, under the limits of what we can do in a small editor' strategy: open the existing file O_RDWR|O_CREAT (mode 0644), ftruncate() it to the new content length, then a single write() of the whole in-memory buffer. Any failure at open/ftruncate/write jumps via 'goto writeerr' to a shared cleanup block that frees the buffer, closes the fd if it was opened, and reports 'Can't save! I/O error: %s' (strerror(errno)) through the status-message bar rather than exiting the program.

editorSave()'s failure path does not attempt to restore the file's previous on-disk contents; ftruncate() happens before the write(), so a save that truncates successfully but then fails on write() (e.g. disk full, ENOSPC) can leave the on-disk file shorter than either the old or the new version. Kilo keeps the in-memory buffer (and E.dirty left at 1) so the user can retry the save, but the on-disk file itself is not guaranteed intact if the write step fails midway.

Quitting is gated by a dirty-file confirmation: editorProcessKeypress keeps a static int quit_times initialized to KILO_QUIT_TIMES (3). On Ctrl-Q, if E.dirty is nonzero and quit_times is still nonzero, it shows a warning ('Press Ctrl-Q %d more times to quit') and decrements quit_times without quitting; only once quit_times reaches 0 (or the file isn't dirty) does it call exit(0). Any other keypress resets quit_times back to KILO_QUIT_TIMES at the end of editorProcessKeypress, so the count only survives consecutive Ctrl-Q presses.

Ctrl-C is explicitly caught and ignored in editorProcessKeypress with the comment 'We ignore ctrl-c, it can't be so simple to lose the changes to the edited file' — raw mode already disables ISIG so the terminal wouldn't deliver SIGINT anyway, but the switch case is there for defense/clarity.

Both editorProcessKeypress's exit(0) (Ctrl-Q) and editorReadKey's exit(1) (read error) route through the C runtime's normal exit() function, which runs every atexit()-registered handler — including editorAtExit — before the process actually terminates, so raw mode is always restored on these paths as long as enableRawMode() already ran.

atexit()-registered functions are only invoked on normal termination (a call to exit(3), or falling off the end of main()); a process killed by an unhandled signal (e.g. SIGSEGV from a real bug, or SIGKILL) does not run them, so a crash from a genuine memory-safety bug elsewhere in the program can still leave the user's terminal in raw mode with no automatic restoration.

Kilo installs a SIGWINCH handler (handleSigWinCh, wired up with signal(SIGWINCH, handleSigWinCh) inside initEditor()) that re-queries the terminal size via updateWindowSize(), clamps the cursor position (E.cx/E.cy) back inside the new screen bounds if it fell outside, and calls editorRefreshScreen() to repaint immediately — so resizing the terminal window mid-session is handled as a live event, not just picked up on the next keystroke.

updateWindowSize() itself is treated as fatal if it fails: if getWindowSize() (the ioctl/cursor-position-report fallback from the terminal chapters) returns -1, it calls perror('Unable to query the screen for size (columns / rows)') and exit(1). Because initEditor() (which calls updateWindowSize() once, and installs the SIGWINCH handler) runs before enableRawMode() in main(), the very first call to updateWindowSize() can only fail while the terminal is still in cooked mode; the SIGWINCH-driven path can be reached later, once raw mode is on, in which case editorAtExit() would still run before the process exits.

main() itself validates argument count before touching the terminal at all: if argc != 2 it writes a usage message to stderr with fprintf and calls exit(1) — before initEditor(), before any terminal state has been touched, so there is nothing to clean up on that path either.

pitfalls: Reading kilo.c expecting a unified die()/fatal() helper (as popularized by the snaptoken tutorial derivative) will not find one — antirez's original spreads perror()+exit() calls and goto-based local cleanup across each function that needs them.

It's easy to assume every exit() path restores the terminal; it's true only because every fatal exit() that happens *after* enableRawMode() succeeds is covered by the atexit(editorAtExit) registration, and every exit() that can happen *before* it (argument check, editorOpen's fopen failure, the very first updateWindowSize() call) is provably safe simply because raw mode was never entered yet.

atexit() handlers do not run on abnormal termination by an unhandled signal (SIGSEGV, SIGKILL, a real crash) — so a memory-safety bug elsewhere in this NULL-check-free codebase could still leave a user's shell in raw mode with a blank/broken-looking terminal, requiring `stty sane` or `reset` to recover.

enableRawMode()'s return value is discarded in main(), so a failure to enter raw mode (non-TTY stdin, tcgetattr/tcsetattr failure) is silently swallowed — the editor keeps running in cooked/canonical mode instead of refusing to start, which can look like broken input handling rather than a reported error.

editorSave()'s ftruncate()-then-write() sequence is not atomic/crash-safe: a write() failure partway through (e.g. ENOSPC) after a successful ftruncate() can leave the on-disk file truncated relative to both the old and new contents; Kilo reports the error and leaves E.dirty set, but does not roll the file back or write to a temp file + rename() the way a more defensive editor would.

None of Kilo's malloc()/realloc() calls check for NULL — an out-of-memory condition is not handled at all and would fault on the first subsequent dereference, not report cleanly through editorSetStatusMessage or perror the way file/terminal errors do.

claims.text: kilo.c's own README states the design choice directly: "Kilo does not depend on any library (not even curses). It uses fairly standard VT100 (and similar terminals) escape sequences. The project is in alpha stage and was written in just a few hours."

kilo.c includes only POSIX/Unix headers — termios.h, sys/ioctl.h, sys/types.h, sys/time.h, unistd.h, fcntl.h, and signal.h — for raw-mode terminal control (tcgetattr/tcsetattr), window-size queries (ioctl + TIOCGWINSZ), file descriptors, and SIGWINCH handling. None of these exist on Windows, and the source contains no Windows conditional compilation or compatibility shims of any kind.

Near the top of kilo.c, `#ifdef __linux__ #define _POSIX_C_SOURCE 200809L #endif` guards a single feature-test macro definition that is compiled in only on Linux. This is needed because glibc hides some POSIX.1-2008 interfaces — notably `getline()`, which editorOpen() uses to read the file line by line — behind an explicit opt-in macro when a program is built under strict conformance settings; macOS's libc does not gate the same functions behind that macro, so the guard would be redundant (and is skipped) there. It is the one place in the file where kilo.c explicitly acknowledges that "POSIX" is not perfectly uniform across POSIX-ish systems.

getWindowSize() has two strategies for learning the terminal's size: first `ioctl(1, TIOCGWINSZ, &ws)`, and only if that fails or reports zero columns, a fallback that writes `\x1b[999C\x1b[999B` to push the cursor to the bottom-right corner and then reads its reported position back via the `\x1b[6n` (DSR) escape sequence used by getCursorPosition(). This dual path is itself a portability technique: ioctl's TIOCGWINSZ is the fast, direct answer on a real terminal device, but it can be unavailable or return 0 in some non-standard terminal setups, so the escape-sequence probe is a second, more universally-supported way to ask the same question of anything that merely speaks VT100.

getWindowSize() takes an `ofd` parameter for the output file descriptor to query, but its ioctl call hardcodes the literal file descriptor `1` (`ioctl(1, TIOCGWINSZ, &ws)`) instead of using `ofd`. This only works because of the POSIX convention that a process's file descriptor 1 is its standard output unless a caller has explicitly redirected it — an implicit assumption baked into a single hardcoded integer rather than something the function's own signature enforces.

Kilo emits raw VT100/ANSI escape sequences directly (cursor movement, screen clear/erase, SGR color codes, DECTCEM cursor show/hide) with no terminfo/termcap database and no library like ncurses in between. This means Kilo needs nothing installed or linked to run, and works on any terminal emulator that happens to understand the specific VT100-family sequences it hardcodes (xterm, most Linux terminal emulators, macOS Terminal.app/iTerm2) — but unlike a terminfo-backed program, it has no fallback or capability-negotiation path for a terminal that doesn't understand one of those sequences (a bare serial VT52, TERM=dumb, some minimal embedded consoles).

Kilo treats file content as an opaque byte stream with no Unicode/UTF-8 awareness. Non-printable bytes are detected with the C-locale-dependent `isprint()` and substituted with a placeholder glyph (HL_NONPRINT) rather than decoded as part of a multi-byte character; horizontal-space rendering and cursor-column math assume one byte equals one screen column. A multi-byte UTF-8 character (anything outside 7-bit ASCII) is therefore rendered and measured as two or more separate single-width columns rather than as one character, which will visibly misalign the cursor and rendered text on any file containing non-ASCII text.

Long lines are never soft-wrapped. Instead, `E.coloff` (the horizontal scroll offset) shifts the rendered window sideways as the cursor moves past `E.screencols`, the same mechanism `editorMoveCursor` and `editorFind` both already use for vertical scrolling's horizontal counterpart. A line longer than the terminal is wide is viewed by scrolling right, never by folding it onto a second visual row.

Kilo has no undo/redo of any kind, no cut/copy/paste to or from the operating system clipboard (there is no clipboard-related code in kilo.c at all), no multiple buffers or split windows (one file per process, set once from argv[1] and never reassigned), and no mouse support — every interaction in editorProcessKeypress() is keyboard-only.

Every tunable in kilo.c is a compile-time C constant, not a runtime config file or command-line flag: the tab stop is hardcoded to 8 columns (`tabs*8` in editorUpdateRow, with no KILO_TAB_STOP-style override), the incremental-search query buffer is `KILO_QUERY_LEN` (256) bytes, the status-message buffer is a fixed 80 bytes, and the unsaved-changes quit confirmation is `KILO_QUIT_TIMES` (3) presses. Changing any of them means editing the source and recompiling.

Kilo's compile-time language-highlighting database (HLDB, covered in Chapter 16) ships with exactly one populated entry, for C/C++ — every other language a reader might want highlighted is a limitation of the shipped binary, not the mechanism, since HLDB is designed to be extended by adding more struct editorSyntax entries.

kilo.c uses the GCC/Clang-specific `__attribute__((unused))` extension (on handleSigWinCh's unused signal-number parameter) with no portable fallback — another small, concrete way the source assumes a POSIX-y compiler toolchain (gcc/clang) rather than aiming for strict, compiler-agnostic ISO C portability.

pitfalls: Assuming kilo.c is 'just C' and therefore trivially portable to Windows — every terminal-control, signal, and file-descriptor call it makes is POSIX-specific, with zero conditional compilation for a non-POSIX target.
Confusing 'doesn't link ncurses' with 'terminal-capability portable' — no-library-dependency and no-terminfo-fallback are different axes; Kilo is generous on the first and has nothing on the second.

Editing or testing kilo.c only with ASCII source files — the byte-per-column assumption means a UTF-8 file with any non-ASCII character will misrender and can desync cursor position from what's visually under it.

Reading the single-language HLDB as a hard ceiling rather than a populated-with-one-example table — Chapter 16 shows the mechanism supports more languages; the limitation is what ships, not what the code can do.

claims.text: kilo.c is built by a three-line Makefile as a single translation unit with no header files at all: `all: kilo` / `kilo: kilo.c` / `$(CC) -o kilo kilo.c -Wall -W -pedantic -std=c99` / `clean: rm kilo`. There is no separate compilation, no .h files to keep in sync, and no build-system complexity whatsoever — the entire program is one file in, one binary out.

A direct count of top-level function definitions in the shipped kilo.c (via grep on the downloaded source) finds 34 functions, all in one file and one C namespace: terminal control (enableRawMode, disableRawMode, editorAtExit, editorReadKey, getCursorPosition, getWindowSize, updateWindowSize, handleSigWinCh), the row/buffer model (is_separator, editorRowHasOpenComment, editorUpdateRow, editorInsertRow, editorFreeRow, editorDelRow, editorRowsToString, editorRowInsertChar, editorRowAppendString, editorRowDelChar), syntax highlighting (editorUpdateSyntax, editorSyntaxToColor, editorSelectSyntaxHighlight), editing operations (editorInsertChar, editorInsertNewline, editorDelChar), file I/O (editorOpen, editorSave), screen/output (abAppend, abFree, editorRefreshScreen, editorSetStatusMessage), search (editorFind), input dispatch (editorMoveCursor, editorProcessKeypress), and orchestration (initEditor, editorFileWasModified, main).

Those 8 functional groups already line up, almost one-to-one, with this book's own Part II-V chapter boundaries (the terminal & the loop, data & navigation, files, search & highlighting) — the natural seams a refactor would cut along already exist as a table of contents, before a single line of kilo.c has been moved.

editorProcessKeypress's dispatch is one switch over 12 case groups (ENTER, CTRL_C, CTRL_Q, CTRL_S, CTRL_F, the BACKSPACE/CTRL_H/DEL_KEY trio, PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN, the four ARROW_* keys, CTRL_L, ESC, and default) sitting inside a single ~70-line function; PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN's case body itself contains a small nested loop that repeatedly calls editorMoveCursor, and the default case is what makes an ordinary typed character actually insert text (editorInsertChar(c)).

The antirez/kilo repository has no automated tests and no CI configuration of any kind: a direct check for common test/CI paths (.travis.yml, .github/workflows, tests/, test/, kilo.spec, test.c, CMakeLists.txt) returns HTTP 404 for every one of them. Any refactor of this code — moving a function, splitting a file, changing a signature — currently has no automated way to confirm behavior didn't change; the only check available is running the editor by hand.

Martin Fowler's Catalog of Refactorings defines "refactoring" specifically as a disciplined sequence of small, behavior-preserving code transformations, each one verified before the next is attempted — not a synonym for "rewrite." Two of its named techniques map directly onto kilo.c's shape: Extract Function (pulling a cohesive chunk of a large function out under its own name, a candidate for editorProcessKeypress's PAGE_UP/PAGE_DOWN case body) and Replace Conditional with Polymorphism, whose C-idiomatic cousin — since C has no classes — is a dispatch table: an array or lookup mapping each key code to a function pointer, replacing the switch's case labels with data.

General C-refactoring literature treats "move state out of a global and into an explicit, passed argument" as a specific, well-studied transformation, motivated by the same information-hiding argument used against global variables generally: a function that reads and writes a global can't be tested in isolation or run twice with two different states in the same process, while a function that takes its state as a parameter can be — the tradeoff is that every call site gains one more argument to thread through.

kilo.c's Makefile already opts every build into `-Wall -W -pedantic -std=c99` — compiler warnings and strict-standard checking are cheap, already-present maintainability tooling that predates and is independent of any structural refactor; it catches a different class of problem (type mismatches, unused values, non-standard extensions) than reorganizing functions into modules ever would.

kilo.c's own error-cleanup idiom — a local `goto fatal:` or `goto writeerr:` label that a function jumps to from several failure points to run one shared cleanup block before returning — is itself a recognized, idiomatic C pattern for single-exit-point error handling, not automatically a maintainability smell; Chapter 17 already established that the actual gap in kilo.c is the *inconsistency* across call sites (no shared die() helper unifying them), which is a different problem from the goto construct itself.

pitfalls: Treating 'refactor' as a synonym for 'rewrite': Fowler's definition is specifically small, behavior-preserving steps, each checked before the next — and kilo.c has no automated tests to check against, so that promise can only be kept by careful manual re-verification after every step, not assumed.

Assuming splitting one file into several is free. Real costs: header files to keep in sync, deciding whether struct editorConfig / erow are exposed across the new boundary or hidden behind opaque pointers (extra indirection either way), and losing the 'read the whole editor in one sitting' property Chapter 1 named as Kilo's actual selling point.

Reading kilo.c's goto-based cleanup labels (goto fatal, goto writeerr) as a bug or a smell to remove — it's an idiomatic, single-exit-point C error-handling pattern. The real, already-established gap (Chapter 17) is that each call site improvises its own version instead of sharing one helper, not the goto construct itself.

Reaching for a dispatch table (or any structural change to editorProcessKeypress) before there's a way to confirm every key still does the same thing afterward — that verification harness is exactly what Chapter 21 (Testing a Terminal App) builds; doing it first, or at least alongside, is the standard order of operations, not an afterthought.
